sentences.base_language: What are you going to buy?
sentences.grammarNote: 〜(으)ㄹ 거예요 (geoyeyo) expresses future plans or intentions: 살 거예요 (going to buy), 먹을 거예요 (going to eat). It's the everyday future tense in Korean — much like 'going to' in English, signaling a planned action.

sentences.base_language: I'm going to buy vegetables and fruit.
sentences.grammarNote: 랑/이랑 (rang/irang) = 'and / with', used in casual speech to list things or people. 과일이랑 채소 (fruit and vegetables). The more formal equivalents are 와/과 (wa/gwa). Like 을/를, the choice depends on whether the preceding syllable ends in a vowel or consonant.

개 (gae) is the general counter for individual objects: 한 개 (one), 두 개 (two). Korean won (원) is the currency. Prices at traditional markets are often negotiable — which leads to the next very useful phrase.

sentences.base_language: Can you give me a discount?
sentences.grammarNote: 깎아 주세요 (kkakka juseyo) = 'please give a discount / knock off a little'. 깎다 (kkakda) means to cut or reduce. 좀 (jom) softens the request. At traditional markets (재래시장) this kind of bargaining is expected and natural.

sentences.base_language: I also want to buy kimchi.
sentences.grammarNote: 도 (do) = 'also / too', placed directly after the word it modifies: 김치도 (kimchi too). 〜고 싶어요 (go sipeoyo) = 'want to do': 사고 싶어요 (want to buy), 먹고 싶어요 (want to eat). Combine them for 'also want to buy'.
